stem:
A 17-year-old presents with anorexia nervosa, restrictive subtype. Her BMI is 13.5 kg/m^2. She has lost 11 kg in three months. She has been eating around 600 kcal a day. Bloods: phosphate 0.6 mmol/L (0.8-1.5), potassium 3.0 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), magnesium 0.6 mmol/L (0.7-1.0), albumin 28 g/L (35-50). ECG shows bradycardia with QTc 480 ms (normal less than 450). She is medically admitted overnight.

The paediatric and dietetic teams are planning refeeding.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 38, BP 88/52 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 35.6°C
data_table.Investigations: Phosphate 0.6 mmol/L, potassium 3.0 mmol/L, magnesium 0.6 mmol/L, albumin 28 g/L, ECG QTc 480 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial refeeding plan?

options.text: 1400-1800 kcal/day with phosphate, potassium, magnesium, and thiamine cover
options.rationale: AU MARSIPAN and ANZAED guidance now favour higher starting calorie ranges (1400-2000 kcal/day) with proactive electrolyte and thiamine cover, rather than the historic 500-1000 kcal/day. (Source: ANZAED Eating Disorders Practice Standards 2022)

options.text: Start oral feeding at 500 kcal/day to avoid refeeding syndrome
options.rationale: Historic 500 kcal/day under-feeds severe AN and prolongs starvation; current AU guidelines support higher calorie start with electrolyte cover. (Source: ANZAED Eating Disorders Practice Standards 2022)

options.text: Insert NGT and feed at full maintenance without electrolyte correction
options.rationale: Feeding without replacement of phosphate and other electrolytes precipitates refeeding syndrome with arrhythmia and seizures. (Source: ANZAED Eating Disorders Practice Standards 2022)

options.text: Start IV dextrose 10% infusion without thiamine for rapid calorie delivery
options.rationale: Dextrose without prior thiamine in malnourished patients precipitates Wernicke encephalopathy; thiamine must precede any carbohydrate load. (Source: ANZAED Eating Disorders Practice Standards 2022)

options.text: Discharge home with weekly outpatient dietitian review only
options.rationale: Bradycardia, electrolyte abnormalities, prolonged QTc, and BMI 13.5 are medical admission criteria; outpatient is unsafe. (Source: ANZAED Eating Disorders Practice Standards 2022)

explanation.summary: Severe anorexia with bradycardia, electrolyte abnormalities, and a low BMI requires medical admission. Refeeding syndrome (phosphate, potassium, magnesium drop, thiamine depletion, cardiac decompensation) historically led to ultra-low starting calories. Current AU MARSIPAN and ANZAED guidance favours higher starting calories (1400-2000 kcal/day) combined with proactive electrolyte replacement and thiamine 100 mg IV or oral prior to any carbohydrate load. Cardiac monitoring is required during the first week.
key_points: Higher start (1400-2000 kcal/day) with electrolyte and thiamine cover
Thiamine before any carbohydrate
Cardiac monitoring during first week
explanation.pearls: Phosphate below 0.5 mmol/L is the strongest predictor of refeeding-related arrhythmia; replace IV with 9 mmol over 4-6 hours and recheck.

stem:
A 22-year-old presented three months ago with first-episode psychosis precipitated by daily high-potency cannabis use. He was started on risperidone 4 mg and stopped cannabis at admission. His positive symptoms (persecutory delusions, third-person auditory hallucinations) have persisted despite three months of medication adherence and confirmed abstinence on urine toxicology. He has impaired functioning at university and social withdrawal. There is no family history of psychotic illness. He has not had a manic or depressive episode. No other substance use is identified.

The early psychosis team is reviewing the diagnosis.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78, BP 122/74 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Flat affect, residual delusions of persecution, third-person hallucinations
data_table.Investigations: Urine cannabinoid negative on three samples; routine bloods, MRI brain normal

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis at this stage?

options.text: Schizophrenia (cannabis was a precipitant in a vulnerable individual)
options.rationale: Substance-induced psychosis is expected to resolve with abstinence; persistence at 12 weeks with confirmed abstinence and ongoing functional decline shifts the diagnosis to a primary psychotic disorder. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP Early Psychosis)

options.text: Cannabis-induced psychotic disorder, currently ongoing course
options.rationale: Substance-induced psychosis by DSM-5 resolves within 4 weeks of abstinence; 12 weeks of persistent psychosis with confirmed abstinence excludes this. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP Early Psychosis)

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder lasting longer than one month
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder is by definition under 1 month with full return to baseline; this case has lasted 3 months with ongoing impairment. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP Early Psychosis)

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder, depressive subtype currently
options.rationale: Schizoaffective requires a major mood episode concurrent with psychosis; no mood episode is described. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP Early Psychosis)

options.text: Schizophreniform disorder still within the 6-month diagnostic window
options.rationale: Schizophreniform is the correct provisional label between 1 and 6 months only when the clinician cannot yet exclude full schizophrenia trajectory; at 3 months with marked functional decline a schizophrenia diagnosis is increasingly likely. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP Early Psychosis)

explanation.summary: Substance-induced psychotic disorder by DSM-5 is a time-limited diagnosis that resolves within approximately one month of abstinence. Persistence beyond this point shifts the formulation toward a primary psychotic illness, with cannabis having acted as a precipitant in a vulnerable individual. Cannabis use approximately doubles schizophrenia risk in vulnerable individuals; high-potency forms further increase risk. Long-term antipsychotic treatment and early psychosis service involvement are recommended.
key_points: Substance-induced psychosis resolves within ~4 weeks of abstinence
Persistence beyond shifts diagnosis to primary disorder
Cannabis is precipitant in vulnerable people
explanation.pearls: Schizophreniform is a provisional 1-6 month placeholder; reserve for cases where you genuinely cannot predict whether the episode will remit, and re-formulate at 6 months.

stem:
A 30-year-old woman with a documented history of bipolar I disorder (one previous postpartum manic episode three years ago requiring inpatient care) has just delivered her second baby at 39+5. She stopped her lithium six months ago to plan pregnancy and has not restarted. On day 4 postpartum she is irritable, sleeping 1 hour per night, hyperverbal, has religious delusions about the baby, and is intermittently confused. She is breastfeeding. She is currently calm but the family are very concerned. She does not believe she is unwell.

The perinatal team has been called urgently.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 102, BP 128/78 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99%, temperature 37.0°C
data_table.Examination: Pressured speech, religious delusions involving baby, intermittent confusion, MMSE 24/30
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH, calcium, magnesium, infection screen within normal limits; MRI brain not yet done

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial step?

options.text: Admit to mother-baby unit, olanzapine 5-10 mg, lorazepam PRN, plus brain imaging
options.rationale: Postpartum psychosis is a psychiatric emergency requiring inpatient care, preferably MBU; antipsychotic plus benzodiazepine is first-line, and confusion mandates exclusion of organic mimic. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health 2023)

options.text: Restart lithium 250 mg twice daily and discharge with community follow-up
options.rationale: Outpatient management of postpartum psychosis is unsafe given infanticide and suicide risks; admission is required. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health 2023)

options.text: Reassure as normal postpartum baby blues and observe at home overnight
options.rationale: Baby blues do not present with delusions, hyperactivity, sleeplessness, or confusion; this is psychosis. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health 2023)

options.text: Cease breastfeeding immediately as a prerequisite to any medication
options.rationale: Many psychotropics are compatible with breastfeeding; cessation is a separate decision balanced with treatment needs, not a prerequisite. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health 2023)

options.text: Initiate ECT same day before any pharmacological trial at all
options.rationale: ECT is rapidly effective in postpartum psychosis but is reserved for severe cases or non-response after a short pharmacological trial; antipsychotic plus benzodiazepine is the typical first step. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health 2023)

explanation.summary: Postpartum psychosis affects 1-2 per 1000 deliveries but recurrence risk in bipolar I with a prior postpartum episode is 25-50%. Stopping mood stabilisers around delivery increases relapse risk substantially. Admission to a mother-baby unit allows treatment while preserving attachment, with antipsychotic plus benzodiazepine as the first pharmacological step. Lithium can be restarted with monitoring, and ECT is reserved for severe or treatment-resistant cases. Prominent confusion mandates organic workup including thyroid, infection, and consideration of autoimmune encephalitis.
key_points: PPP in bipolar I is high-risk: mood stabiliser at delivery prevents relapse
MBU admission preserves mother-infant attachment
Antipsychotic + benzodiazepine is the typical first regimen
explanation.pearls: Postpartum psychosis with prominent confusion warrants exclusion of NMDA receptor encephalitis - send anti-NMDA antibodies and consider EEG.

sources.label: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

stem:
A 64-year-old man presents with sudden tearing interscapular pain radiating to the chest 45 minutes ago. He has uncontrolled hypertension. Examination shows a blood pressure difference of 28 mmHg between arms. There is a faint early diastolic murmur at the left sternal edge. He has a strong pulse in both groins. Chest X-ray shows a widened mediastinum. ECG shows sinus rhythm with left ventricular hypertrophy.

He is haemodynamically stable.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 92, BP 178/108 mmHg right arm, 150/96 mmHg left arm, RR 22, SpO2 96%, temperature 36.8°C
data_table.Examination: Faint early diastolic murmur left sternal edge, equal groin pulses
data_table.Investigations: CXR widened mediastinum; ECG sinus rhythm LVH; troponin pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first investigation?

options.text: Contrast-enhanced CT angiogram chest, abdomen, and pelvis
options.rationale: CT angiography is first-line in stable patients with suspected aortic dissection; it confirms diagnosis, identifies type, and maps branch involvement. (Source: ESC Aortic Diseases 2024)

options.text: Transthoracic echocardiogram at the bedside immediately
options.rationale: TTE has limited sensitivity for type B dissection and does not map branch involvement; it is adjunctive. (Source: ESC Aortic Diseases 2024)

options.text: Transoesophageal echocardiogram in the emergency department
options.rationale: TOE is reserved for haemodynamically unstable patients who cannot be transported for CT; CT is preferred when stable. (Source: ESC Aortic Diseases 2024)

options.text: MR angiogram aorta with non-contrast diffusion sequences
options.rationale: MRA is excellent but slower and less available; CT is first-line in the acute setting. (Source: ESC Aortic Diseases 2024)

options.text: D-dimer for risk stratification before any imaging
options.rationale: D-dimer has high sensitivity but is not used to rule out dissection in high pre-test probability; proceed directly to CT. (Source: ESC Aortic Diseases 2024)

explanation.summary: Acute aortic dissection should be suspected with tearing chest or back pain, hypertension, pulse or BP differential between limbs, or a new aortic regurgitation murmur. CT angiography is first-line in stable patients because it confirms diagnosis, classifies type (Stanford A involves ascending; type B does not), and maps branch vessel involvement for surgical or endovascular planning. TOE is the alternative in unstable patients. Immediate management is heart rate control (IV esmolol or labetalol) targeting HR <60 then SBP <120 mmHg.
key_points: Stable patients: CT angiogram first
Unstable: bedside TOE alternative
Heart rate control before blood pressure
explanation.pearls: Stanford type A involves the ascending aorta and is a surgical emergency; type B is initially medically managed unless complicated by ischaemia, rupture, or refractory pain.

stem:
A 42-year-old presents to ED with 12 hours of fever, severe headache, photophobia, and neck stiffness. She is drowsy (GCS 13). There are no focal neurological signs. She has no rash. She has no immunocompromise. CT brain prior to lumbar puncture is normal. Lumbar puncture shows turbid CSF with neutrophil predominance (WCC 2200 x10^6/L, 95% neutrophils), protein 2.4 g/L (0.15-0.40), glucose 1.4 mmol/L with simultaneous serum glucose 6.0 mmol/L.

The ED registrar has IV access established.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 118, BP 110/70 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 97%, temperature 39.4°C
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, GCS 13, neck stiffness, no rash, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: CSF turbid, WCC 2200 x10^6/L 95% neutrophils, protein 2.4 g/L, glucose 1.4 mmol/L (serum 6.0); CT brain normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate treatment now?

options.text: IV dexamethasone with first dose of ceftriaxone 2 g and benzylpenicillin 2.4 g
options.rationale: AU eTG specifies empiric ceftriaxone plus benzylpenicillin (for Listeria cover in age >50, immunocompromised, or pregnant) with dexamethasone given before or with first antibiotic dose. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Meningitis)

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 2 g alone without any corticosteroid adjunct
options.rationale: Dexamethasone given before or with first antibiotic dose reduces neurological sequelae in S. pneumoniae meningitis; omission is non-standard. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Meningitis)

options.text: IV vancomycin monotherapy while awaiting susceptibility result
options.rationale: Vancomycin monotherapy does not cover Gram-negative or Listeria; ceftriaxone is the empiric backbone. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Meningitis)

options.text: IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg without any antibacterial cover concurrently
options.rationale: CSF neutrophil-predominant with low glucose indicates bacterial meningitis; acyclovir is added if HSV encephalitis suspected, not as monotherapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Meningitis)

options.text: Delay all antibiotics until CSF Gram stain result is available
options.rationale: Empiric antibiotics must be given within 30 minutes of suspicion in bacterial meningitis; Gram stain delay risks death. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Meningitis)

explanation.summary: Suspected bacterial meningitis requires empiric IV antibiotics within 30 minutes. Australian eTG empiric therapy is ceftriaxone 2 g IV plus benzylpenicillin 2.4 g IV if age >50, pregnant, or immunocompromised (Listeria cover). Dexamethasone 10 mg IV is given with or before the first antibiotic dose; it reduces neurological sequelae in pneumococcal meningitis. CT brain is required before lumbar puncture only in patients with focal neurology, seizure, immunocompromise, GCS <12, or papilloedema; LP should not delay antibiotics.
key_points: Empiric ceftriaxone +/- benzylpenicillin within 30 minutes
Dexamethasone with or before first antibiotic dose
Listeria cover in age >50, pregnant, immunocompromised
explanation.pearls: Notify public health within 24 hours of suspected meningococcal disease and offer ciprofloxacin 500 mg single dose to close household contacts within the previous 7 days.

stem:
A 32-year-old presents with one episode of haematemesis 12 hours ago after binge drinking and forceful vomiting. He has had no further bleeding. He has no melaena. He has no comorbidities and takes no regular medications. Bloods: haemoglobin 148 g/L (130-180), urea 5.4 mmol/L (2.5-7.5), creatinine 76 micromol/L (60-110), INR 1.0 (0.9-1.2). Examination is unremarkable. His Glasgow-Blatchford score is 0.

He wishes to go home.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78, BP 124/76 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.6°C
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, no tenderness, no signs of chronic liver disease, no melaena on PR
data_table.Investigations: Haemoglobin 148 g/L, urea 5.4 mmol/L, creatinine 76 micromol/L, INR 1.0

lead_in: What is the most appropriate disposition?

options.text: Discharge home with safety netting plus outpatient gastroscopy
options.rationale: Glasgow-Blatchford score of 0 identifies a very low risk group safely managed as outpatient; AU and BSG guidelines support discharge with outpatient endoscopy. (Source: BSG Upper GI Bleeding 2022)

options.text: Admit and perform urgent inpatient gastroscopy within 4 hours
options.rationale: Urgent gastroscopy is for haemodynamic instability or ongoing bleeding; this patient is stable with GBS 0. (Source: BSG Upper GI Bleeding 2022)

options.text: Admit overnight for observation with serial haemoglobin checks
options.rationale: GBS 0 has very low risk of rebleeding; routine admission is not required. (Source: BSG Upper GI Bleeding 2022)

options.text: Start IV pantoprazole 80 mg bolus then infusion 8 mg/hour as inpatient
options.rationale: IV PPI infusion is for confirmed high-risk ulcer post-endoscopy; not indicated empirically in low-risk GBS 0. (Source: BSG Upper GI Bleeding 2022)

options.text: Transfuse 1 unit packed red cells empirically before discharge
options.rationale: Haemoglobin is normal and there is no active bleeding; transfusion has no indication. (Source: NBA Patient Blood Management)

explanation.summary: The Glasgow-Blatchford score uses urea, haemoglobin, blood pressure, pulse, and clinical features to risk-stratify upper GI bleeds. A score of 0 corresponds to a less than 1% rebleeding risk and these patients can be safely discharged with outpatient endoscopy. Higher scores warrant admission and earlier endoscopy. Mallory-Weiss tear is a typical low-risk diagnosis. PPI infusion is reserved for high-risk ulcers identified at endoscopy.
key_points: GBS 0: outpatient gastroscopy is safe
Higher GBS: admit for inpatient endoscopy
IV PPI infusion after endoscopy, not empirically
explanation.pearls: A urea-to-creatinine ratio above 100 (using urea mmol/L and creatinine micromol/L) suggests an upper rather than lower source of GI bleeding due to absorption of digested blood.

stem:
A 38-year-old woman presents with one week of fatigue, bruising, and intermittent confusion. She has no recent infection. Bloods: haemoglobin 78 g/L (115-165), platelets 18 x10^9/L (150-400), reticulocytes 6.2% (0.5-2.5), LDH 1820 U/L (140-280), unconjugated bilirubin 42 micromol/L (3-21), creatinine 138 micromol/L (45-90). Coombs test negative. Blood film shows 8 schistocytes per high power field. ADAMTS13 activity is sent. INR is 1.0.

The haematology team has been called urgently.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 102, BP 128/78 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99%, temperature 37.4°C
data_table.Examination: Pallor, petechiae, intermittent confusion, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: Haemoglobin 78 g/L, platelets 18 x10^9/L, retics 6.2%, LDH 1820 U/L, bilirubin 42 micromol/L (unconjugated), creatinine 138 micromol/L; Coombs negative; film 8 schistocytes/hpf; INR 1.0

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Plasma exchange and pulse methylprednisolone, awaiting ADAMTS13 result
options.rationale: TTP is a haematological emergency with high untreated mortality; plasma exchange must be started on clinical grounds (microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, neurology) before ADAMTS13. (Source: HSANZ TTP Position Statement)

options.text: Platelet transfusion to platelets >100 x10^9/L before any other intervention
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion is contraindicated in TTP unless life-threatening bleeding; it may worsen thrombosis. (Source: HSANZ TTP Position Statement)

options.text: Cyclophosphamide induction prior to any plasma exchange therapy
options.rationale: Cyclophosphamide is not part of TTP induction; the AU pathway is plasma exchange + steroid +/- rituximab/caplacizumab. (Source: HSANZ TTP Position Statement)

options.text: Eculizumab as first-line for presumed atypical haemolytic uraemic syndrome
options.rationale: Eculizumab is for atypical HUS not TTP; relatively low creatinine and severe neurology with PLASMIC favours TTP. (Source: HSANZ TTP Position Statement)

options.text: Observation pending ADAMTS13 result before further therapy
options.rationale: ADAMTS13 results take days; waiting risks death. Clinical PLASMIC score guides immediate exchange. (Source: HSANZ TTP Position Statement)

explanation.summary: TTP is a thrombotic microangiopathy driven by ADAMTS13 deficiency causing microvascular platelet thrombi, leading to microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, neurological signs, renal impairment, and fever. The PLASMIC score (platelet count, haemolysis markers, malignancy absence, transplant absence, MCV, INR, creatinine) identifies high-risk cases. Treatment is plasma exchange (PEX) with corticosteroid started before ADAMTS13 confirmation; caplacizumab and rituximab are AU-funded adjuncts. Platelet transfusion is contraindicated unless life-threatening bleeding.
key_points: Clinical diagnosis triggers PEX before ADAMTS13
Platelet transfusion contraindicated in TTP
Adjuncts: caplacizumab, rituximab
explanation.pearls: PLASMIC score 6-7 has a positive predictive value >90% for ADAMTS13 <10%; this threshold supports immediate plasma exchange while awaiting confirmatory testing.

stem:
A 48-year-old man with known Addison disease has had gastroenteritis for two days. He vomited his morning hydrocortisone. He now presents drowsy with abdominal pain. Bloods: sodium 124 mmol/L (135-145), potassium 5.7 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), glucose 3.1 mmol/L (3.5-7.8), urea 12.4 mmol/L (2.5-7.5), creatinine 142 micromol/L (60-110). He has hyperpigmentation. He is on usual maintenance hydrocortisone 20-10-10 mg daily and fludrocortisone 100 mcg.

IV access has been obtained.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 124, BP 86/52 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 98%, temperature 37.0°C
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, dry mucous membranes, hyperpigmented buccal mucosa
data_table.Medications: Hydrocortisone 20-10-10 mg daily, fludrocortisone 100 mcg (missed today)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg and 0.9% sodium chloride 1 L over 30 min
options.rationale: Adrenal crisis is treated with IV hydrocortisone 100 mg plus isotonic crystalloid resuscitation; fludrocortisone is not needed acutely as hydrocortisone provides mineralocorticoid activity at stress dose. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: IV dexamethasone 8 mg only with maintenance crystalloid fluid
options.rationale: Dexamethasone lacks mineralocorticoid activity and is reserved for diagnostic settings where Synacthen testing is planned; hydrocortisone is preferred in crisis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Restart oral hydrocortisone 30 mg and observe in ED short stay
options.rationale: Oral therapy is contraindicated in vomiting and shock; parenteral is required. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: IV insulin and dextrose infusion to correct hyperkalaemia first
options.rationale: Hyperkalaemia in adrenal crisis corrects with cortisol replacement and fluid; insulin-dextrose risks hypoglycaemia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Hypertonic 3% sodium chloride 100 mL over 30 minutes for hyponatraemia
options.rationale: Hyponatraemia in adrenal crisis is corrected by hydrocortisone plus isotonic fluid; hypertonic saline risks osmotic demyelination given chronic hyponatraemia of unknown duration. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

explanation.summary: Adrenal crisis presents with hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia, and altered mental state. Triggers include intercurrent illness, vomiting of replacement therapy, and surgery. IV hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus followed by 200 mg/24 hours is the AU standard, with isotonic crystalloid for hypotension. At hydrocortisone doses >=40 mg/day, no separate fludrocortisone is needed. Underlying trigger (gastroenteritis here) must be treated. Patient education and sick-day rules with parenteral hydrocortisone kit at home prevent recurrence.
key_points: Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV + 0.9% NaCl is the AU regimen
No fludrocortisone needed at stress doses
Treat trigger and educate sick-day rules
explanation.pearls: Patients with Addison should carry a Medic-Alert bracelet and a parenteral hydrocortisone 100 mg IM kit; instruct partners and family to administer in vomiting or unconsciousness.

stem:
A 58-year-old man presents with three weeks of haemoptysis, fatigue, sinusitis, and progressive dyspnoea. Creatinine has risen from 78 to 312 micromol/L (60-110) over two weeks. Urinalysis shows 3+ blood and 2+ protein with red cell casts. Chest CT shows bilateral nodular and cavitating lesions. PR3-ANCA is strongly positive at 142 U/mL (normal less than 5). MPO-ANCA is negative. He has no fever, no recent infection, no haemodynamic instability, and no overt pulmonary haemorrhage. Renal biopsy shows pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis.

He has consented to immunosuppression.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 92, BP 138/86 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 95%, temperature 37.0°C
data_table.Examination: Saddle nose deformity, sinus tenderness, bibasal crackles
data_table.Investigations: Creatinine 312 micromol/L (rose from 78), urinalysis 3+ blood 2+ protein red cell casts, PR3-ANCA 142 U/mL, MPO-ANCA negative, CT bilateral nodular cavitating lesions, renal biopsy pauci-immune crescentic GN

lead_in: What is the most appropriate induction regimen?

options.text: IV methylprednisolone pulse for 3 days then rituximab weekly induction
options.rationale: ANCA-associated vasculitis with severe organ-threatening disease is induced with rituximab or cyclophosphamide plus high-dose steroids; rituximab is preferred in PR3-ANCA and avoids cyclophosphamide gonadotoxicity. (Source: KDIGO ANCA Vasculitis 2024)

options.text: Methotrexate 25 mg weekly with prednisolone 30 mg daily induction
options.rationale: Methotrexate is reserved for non-organ-threatening disease; severe renal involvement requires rituximab or cyclophosphamide. (Source: KDIGO ANCA Vasculitis 2024)

options.text: Plasma exchange alone without any concurrent immunosuppression
options.rationale: Plasma exchange is adjunctive in pulmonary haemorrhage or dialysis-dependent renal involvement; monotherapy does not address autoantibody production. (Source: KDIGO ANCA Vasculitis 2024)

options.text: Mycophenolate mofetil 1 g twice daily as monotherapy induction
options.rationale: MMF can be used for milder disease or maintenance; severe organ-threatening disease requires rituximab or cyclophosphamide induction. (Source: KDIGO ANCA Vasculitis 2024)

options.text: Hydroxychloroquine 400 mg daily with prednisolone 60 mg daily
options.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine has no role in ANCA-associated vasculitis induction; it is used in lupus. (Source: KDIGO ANCA Vasculitis 2024)

explanation.summary: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) commonly presents with upper airway, lower airway, and renal disease; PR3-ANCA is the typical serology. Severe organ-threatening disease is induced with rituximab or cyclophosphamide plus pulse and oral corticosteroids. Rituximab is preferred for PR3-ANCA, women of childbearing age, and to avoid cyclophosphamide toxicity. Plasma exchange is adjunctive in alveolar haemorrhage or severe AKI requiring dialysis. Maintenance is rituximab or azathioprine with low-dose steroid.
key_points: Severe AAV: rituximab or cyclophosphamide + steroid
Rituximab preferred for PR3-ANCA
Plasma exchange adjunctive in haemorrhage or dialysis
explanation.pearls: PEXIVAS trial showed plasma exchange did not improve death or ESRD outcomes overall but a 2024 KDIGO update retains it as an option for diffuse alveolar haemorrhage with hypoxia.

stem:
A 68-year-old presents with two weeks of bone pain, fatigue, and progressive confusion. Bloods: haemoglobin 84 g/L (130-180), creatinine 384 micromol/L (60-110), calcium 3.4 mmol/L corrected (2.10-2.55), albumin 28 g/L (35-50), total protein 96 g/L (60-80). Serum protein electrophoresis shows an IgG kappa M-band of 42 g/L. Serum free light chain ratio kappa/lambda 220 (normal 0.26-1.65). Skeletal survey shows lytic lesions in the skull and pelvis. He is hypovolaemic with mild AKI on dipstick proteinuria 2+.

Haematology and renal teams have been consulted.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 108, BP 96/62 mmHg, RR 20, SpO2 97%, temperature 36.8°C
data_table.Investigations: Haemoglobin 84 g/L, creatinine 384 micromol/L, calcium 3.4 mmol/L corrected, total protein 96 g/L, IgG kappa M-band 42 g/L, kappa/lambda ratio 220, skeletal survey lytic lesions

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management priority?

options.text: IV 0.9% sodium chloride 3 L over 4 hours plus bortezomib regimen
options.rationale: Cast nephropathy AKI in myeloma improves with hydration to reduce light chain delivery and prompt anti-myeloma therapy (typically bortezomib-based); both are needed to preserve renal function. (Source: HSANZ Multiple Myeloma Position Statement)

options.text: IV zoledronic acid 4 mg as the immediate priority for hypercalcaemia
options.rationale: Bisphosphonates require renal function correction first; zoledronic acid is held until creatinine improves and hydration is established. (Source: HSANZ Multiple Myeloma Position Statement)

options.text: Pamidronate 90 mg IV without prior fluid resuscitation hydration
options.rationale: Pamidronate has lower renal toxicity than zoledronate but still requires fluid resuscitation first; hypovolaemia is the immediate threat. (Source: HSANZ Multiple Myeloma Position Statement)

options.text: Plasma exchange as monotherapy for free light chain removal
options.rationale: Plasma exchange does not consistently improve renal outcomes in cast nephropathy and is not a substitute for hydration plus anti-myeloma therapy. (Source: HSANZ Multiple Myeloma Position Statement)

options.text: Urgent haemodialysis with high cut-off filter without chemotherapy
options.rationale: High cut-off dialysis without anti-myeloma therapy does not stop new light chain production; combined treatment is the AU standard. (Source: HSANZ Multiple Myeloma Position Statement)

explanation.summary: Myeloma cast nephropathy is the most common cause of AKI in multiple myeloma. Free kappa light chains co-precipitate with Tamm-Horsfall protein causing tubular casts; hypercalcaemia, dehydration, NSAIDs, and contrast worsen injury. AU management combines aggressive isotonic hydration to lower urinary light chain delivery, treatment of hypercalcaemia (initially with hydration; bisphosphonates after renal recovery), and prompt anti-myeloma therapy (typically bortezomib, cyclophosphamide, dexamethasone) to reduce light chain production. Avoid nephrotoxins.
key_points: Hydration + anti-myeloma therapy together preserve renal function
Bisphosphonates held until renal recovery
Plasma exchange not routine in cast nephropathy
explanation.pearls: A serum free light chain reduction of >=50% within 21 days of bortezomib-based induction predicts renal recovery in approximately 80% of cast nephropathy cases.
